title: API Reference
text:
This page describes the main functions available in the wc-sim package.
These functions cover:

Data wrangling
Ranking comparison
Match simulation
Model validation
Parameter tuning

title: API Reference
text:
This page describes the main functions available in the wc-sim package.
These functions cover:

Data wrangling
Ranking comparison
Match simulation
Model validation
Parameter tuning

title: API Reference
text:
calculate_weighted_rating

Description
Builds a weighted rating table by combining FIFA ranking points with historical World Cup titles.


Arguments







Argument
Description




rank_df
DataFrame containing FIFA ranking data. Must include: rank, name, points


titles_df
DataFrame containing World Cup titles. Must include: team, wc_titles


weight
Integer value representing how many points each World Cup title adds to the rating





Returns
A DataFrame with the following columns:



Column




fifa_rank


name


points


wc_titles


weighted_rating


weighted_rank





Notes
This function merges the two datasets and computes a new rating using:
weighted_rating = points + (wc_titles × weight)

title: API Reference
text:
compare_rank_changes

Description
Compares the original FIFA rank with the new weighted rank.


Arguments



Argument
Description




df_weighted
DataFrame returned from calculate_weighted_rating





Returns
A DataFrame with:



Column




name


fifa_rank


weighted_rank


rank_change





Notes
rank_change = fifa_rank − weighted_rank
A positive value means the team improved its position after including historical titles.

title: API Reference
text:
simulate_match

Description
Simulates a single match between two teams.


Arguments



Argument
Description




team_a
Name of the first team


team_b
Name of the second team


df_weighted
DataFrame containing weighted ratings


random_state
Optional integer seed for reproducibility





Returns
A string with the name of the winning team.


Notes
The probability of team A winning is:
P(A wins) = rating_A / (rating_A + rating_B)
A random number is used to determine the winner.

title: API Reference
text:
simulate_match_n

Description
Simulates a match multiple times and returns win counts and percentages.


Arguments



Argument
Description




team_a
Name of the first team


team_b
Name of the second team


df_weighted
DataFrame containing weighted ratings


n
Number of simulations


random_state
Optional seed for reproducibility





Returns
A DataFrame with:



Column




team


wins


win_pct





Notes
This function is useful for estimating probabilities more reliably than a single simulation.

title: API Reference
text:
validate_weighted_rating

Description
Evaluates how well the weighted rating predicts match outcomes compared to raw FIFA points.


Arguments







Argument
Description




results_df
DataFrame containing historical match results. Must include: home_team, away_team, home_score, away_score


df_weighted
DataFrame with FIFA points and weighted ratings





Returns
A DataFrame with:



Column




metric


correct_predictions


total_matches


accuracy_pct





Notes
The function:

Filters matches where both teams are known
Removes draws
Compares actual winners to predicted winners
Calculates accuracy for both metrics

title: API Reference
text:
tune_weight

Description
Tests multiple title weight values and evaluates their prediction accuracy.


Arguments



Argument
Description




results_df
Historical match results


rank_df
FIFA ranking data


titles_df
World Cup title data


weights
List of weight values to test





Returns
A DataFrame with:



Column




weight


correct_predictions


total_matches


accuracy_pct





Notes
This function helps answer the question:

How much should historical World Cup success influence team ratings?

title: API Reference
text:
Summary
The wc-sim package provides a simple and modular workflow for:

Building a custom rating system
Comparing ranking systems
Simulating match outcomes
Validating predictions
Testing model parameters

Each function is designed to be easy to use and combine with others in a full analysis pipeline.

title: Tutorial
text:
This tutorial shows how to install and use the wc-sim package.
The package helps you:

Load FIFA ranking data and World Cup title data
Create a custom weighted rating
Compare how teams move in the rankings
Simulate head-to-head matches
Validate the rating against historical match results
Test multiple title weights

title: Tutorial
text:
This tutorial shows how to install and use the wc-sim package.
The package helps you:

Load FIFA ranking data and World Cup title data
Create a custom weighted rating
Compare how teams move in the rankings
Simulate head-to-head matches
Validate the rating against historical match results
Test multiple title weights

title: Tutorial
text:
Installation
Clone the repository:
git clone https://github.com/santiago28b/game-predictor.git
cd game-predictor
Install the package locally:
pip install -e .
If you also want development tools like testing and Quarto support, run:
pip install -e .[dev]

title: Tutorial
text:
Load the Data
First, import pandas and the main package function.
import pandas as pd
from wc_sim import calculate_weighted_rating
Now load the ranking and title data from the data folder.
ranks = pd.read_csv("data/rankings.csv")
titles = pd.read_csv("data/titles.csv")
You can inspect the first few rows:
print(ranks.head())
print(titles.head())
The rankings table should contain:



Column




rank


name


points



The titles table should contain:



Column




team


wc_titles

title: Tutorial
text:
Build the Weighted Rating Table
Create the weighted rating table:
df_weighted = calculate_weighted_rating(ranks, titles, weight=50)
print(df_weighted.head())
This function combines current FIFA points with historical World Cup titles.
The idea is:
weighted_rating = FIFA points + (wc_titles × weight)
The output includes:



Column




fifa_rank


name


points


wc_titles


weighted_rating


weighted_rank

title: Tutorial
text:
Compare Rank Changes
Check which teams move the most after including World Cup titles.
from wc_sim import compare_rank_changes

changes = compare_rank_changes(df_weighted)
print(changes.head(10))
This produces:



Column




name


fifa_rank


weighted_rank


rank_change



A positive rank_change means the team improved in the ranking.

title: Tutorial
text:
Simulate One Match
Simulate a single match between two teams.
from wc_sim import simulate_match

winner = simulate_match("Brazil", "Argentina", df_weighted, random_state=42)
print(winner)
This returns the name of the winning team.
The probability is computed as:
P(A wins) = rating_A / (rating_A + rating_B)

title: Tutorial
text:
Simulate Many Matches
Simulate many matches for better estimates.
from wc_sim import simulate_match_n

results = simulate_match_n("Brazil", "Argentina", df_weighted, n=1000, random_state=42)
print(results)
The result includes:



Column




team


wins


win_pct



Example output:



Team
Wins
Win %




Brazil
560
56%


Argentina
440
44%

title: Tutorial
text:
Validate the Weighted Rating
Test whether the weighted rating predicts outcomes better than FIFA points.
from wc_sim import validate_weighted_rating

results_df = pd.read_csv("path_to_your_results_dataset.csv")
summary = validate_weighted_rating(results_df, df_weighted)
print(summary)
The results dataset must contain:



Column




home_team


away_team


home_score


away_score



The output compares prediction accuracy using:

FIFA points
Weighted rating

title: Tutorial
text:
Test Multiple Weights
Instead of choosing one weight, test several.
from wc_sim import tune_weight

weight_results = tune_weight(
    results_df,
    ranks,
    titles,
    weights=[0, 25, 50, 75, 100]
)

print(weight_results)
This shows accuracy for each weight and helps justify which weight to use.

title: Tutorial
text:
Run the Streamlit App
Run the app with:
streamlit run app.py
The app allows users to:

Choose two teams
Set the title weight
Run simulations
Compare ratings
Explore rank changes

title: Tutorial
text:
Typical Workflow
Step 1: Load data
import pandas as pd
from wc_sim import calculate_weighted_rating

ranks = pd.read_csv("data/rankings.csv")
titles = pd.read_csv("data/titles.csv")
results_df = pd.read_csv("data/path_to_your_results_dataset.csv")
Step 2: Build rating
df_weighted = calculate_weighted_rating(ranks, titles, weight=50)
Step 3: Compare ranks
from wc_sim import compare_rank_changes

changes = compare_rank_changes(df_weighted)
print(changes.head())
Step 4: Validate model
from wc_sim import validate_weighted_rating

summary = validate_weighted_rating(results_df, df_weighted)
print(summary)
Step 5: Simulate matches
from wc_sim import simulate_match_n

sim_results = simulate_match_n("Brazil", "Germany", df_weighted, n=5000, random_state=42)
print(sim_results)
Step 6: Tune weight
from wc_sim import tune_weight

best_weights = tune_weight(results_df, ranks, titles, weights=[0, 25, 50, 75, 100])
print(best_weights)

title: Tutorial
text:
Notes and Limitations
This model is simple and interpretable.
It does not include:

Draw probability
Home-field advantage
Injuries
Recent form beyond rankings
Advanced metrics like expected goals


It should be used as a simple analytical tool, not a full prediction system.

title: Tutorial
text:
Summary
In this tutorial you learned how to:

Install the package
Load data
Build the weighted rating
Compare rankings
Simulate matches
Validate results
Test different weights
Run the Streamlit app

For more details, see the Technical Report.

title: Game Predictor
text:
wc-sim is a Python package for simulating international soccer matches using a custom rating system.
The main idea is simple:

start with current FIFA ranking points
add a bonus based on historical FIFA World Cup titles
validate whether this new rating predicts match outcomes better than raw FIFA points
use the rating to simulate head-to-head matches

title: Game Predictor
text:
wc-sim is a Python package for simulating international soccer matches using a custom rating system.
The main idea is simple:

start with current FIFA ranking points
add a bonus based on historical FIFA World Cup titles
validate whether this new rating predicts match outcomes better than raw FIFA points
use the rating to simulate head-to-head matches

title: Game Predictor
text:
Motivating Question
Can we build a more useful World Cup-style match simulator by combining a team’s current FIFA strength with its historical tournament success?

title: Game Predictor
text:
Project Components
This project includes:

a Python package for data wrangling, simulation, and validation
a Streamlit app for head-to-head match simulation
a tutorial for installation and package use
a technical report explaining the methodology and results

title: Game Predictor
text:
Quick Links

Tutorial
Technical Report
API Reference
Streamlit App
GitHub Repository

title: Game Predictor
text:
Main Functions
The package currently includes these main functions:

calculate_weighted_rating()
compare_rank_changes()
simulate_match()
simulate_match_n()
validate_weighted_rating()
tune_weight()

title: Game Predictor
text:
Data Sources
This project uses:

current FIFA rankings
a curated table of World Cup title counts
historical international match results for validation

The data collection and cleaning workflow is documented in the repository, including the notebook used to assemble the dataset.

title: Technical Report
text:
Can we improve an international soccer match simulator by combining current FIFA points with historical FIFA World Cup success?
Most rating systems focus mainly on present performance. This project tests whether adding a small historical prestige component can improve prediction accuracy for international match outcomes.

title: Technical Report
text:
Can we improve an international soccer match simulator by combining current FIFA points with historical FIFA World Cup success?
Most rating systems focus mainly on present performance. This project tests whether adding a small historical prestige component can improve prediction accuracy for international match outcomes.

title: Technical Report
text:
Data Sources
This project uses three main data sources:

Current FIFA rankings with team names and point totals

World Cup title counts for nations that have won the tournament

Historical international match results used to validate the model

The ranking and titles data are stored in the data/ folder. Historical results are used during validation. The repository also includes a notebook documenting the data collection process.

title: Technical Report
text:
Data Wrangling
The main wrangling step is merging current FIFA rankings with historical World Cup title counts.
The weighted rating is defined as:
[ = + ( ) ]
This creates a new score that keeps current performance as the main signal while still allowing historical success to have some influence.

title: Technical Report
text:
Analysis Workflow
The project includes three main analysis steps:

1. Build a weighted ranking table
The package creates a table with:

FIFA rank
FIFA points
World Cup titles
weighted rating
weighted rank



2. Validate the rating
Historical match results are used to compare:

prediction accuracy from raw FIFA points
prediction accuracy from weighted ratings

For each match, the higher-rated team is treated as the predicted winner.


3. Simulate matches
For two teams A and B, the package estimates the probability that team A wins as:
[ P(A ) = ]
Then the package simulates one match or many matches using this probability.

title: Technical Report
text:
Key Takeaways
This project shows that it is possible to build a simple and interpretable simulator from a small custom dataset.
The main strengths are:

easy-to-understand rating formula
reproducible workflow
direct comparison of raw FIFA points vs weighted ratings
interactive Streamlit app

title: Technical Report
text:
Added Improvement: Weight Tuning
A useful extension in this version is testing multiple title weights instead of assuming one fixed value.
This helps answer a better question:
How much should World Cup history matter?
By comparing accuracy across several candidate weights, the user can justify the final choice more clearly.

title: Technical Report
text:
Limitations
This model is intentionally simple. It does not include:

home-field advantage
draw probability
recent roster changes
injuries
match importance
expected goals or shot-based features

Because of this, the simulator should be understood as a lightweight analytical tool, not a full forecasting system.

title: Technical Report
text:
Conclusion
The project meets the main goals of a data science workflow:

collect or assemble data
clean and merge it
create analytical functions in a package
validate a model
communicate results through documentation, a tutorial, a report, and an app

Even with a simple formula, the package provides a clear and reproducible framework for exploring how historical success can affect match prediction.
